worth_it_explanation: Frequency and exclusion controls are some of the fastest-payback checks in an Amazon DSP (demand-side platform) account. An uncapped line item can serve the same person dozens of times in a week with no measurable return. A suppression list that never gets applied keeps burning budget on audiences who already converted or who are disqualified by geography. Neither problem requires a campaign rebuild to fix: adjusting a cap or uploading a suppression audience takes minutes. The checks here surface the three failure modes that waste the most impressions on this platform.
intro: Amazon DSP applies frequency caps and audience exclusions at the order level, which means the controls sit above the individual line items that buy impressions. When a practitioner sets a cap on a line item instead, the order as a whole has no ceiling and total user exposure across line items runs unchecked. The same logic applies to exclusions: a suppression audience attached only to one line item leaves the others free to retarget the same converted or irrelevant users. These checks confirm that the order-level controls are in place, that suppression lists reflect current campaign logic, and that targeting layers have not been stacked so tightly that the line item cannot spend.

audit_checks.title: Frequency caps are set at the line-item level only, leaving order-level exposure uncapped
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When each line item carries its own cap but the order has none, the same user can receive impressions from every line item in the order up to each item's individual ceiling. A user hit by three line items each capped at five impressions per week receives up to fifteen in practice. The excess impressions rarely produce additional conversions and consume budget that could reach new users.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP (demand-side platform) applies frequency caps at two levels: the order and the line item. An order-level cap limits how many times a single user sees any ad from the entire order in a given time window, regardless of how many line items are running beneath it. A line-item cap limits exposure from that specific line item only.

When caps exist only at the line-item level, the order has no aggregate ceiling. A user can accumulate impressions from every active line item in the order, each up to its own cap. In an order with several line items targeting overlapping audiences, total user exposure can be several times higher than any single cap implies.

A few signs point to this problem:
  • The order settings show no frequency cap, while individual line items each carry one
  • Reach-and-frequency reporting shows a small core of users accounting for a disproportionate share of impressions
  • Audience overlap between line items in the same order is high
You may want to set an order-level cap that reflects the total exposure the campaign should deliver per user per week, then keep line-item caps as secondary guardrails for specific placements or formats. The Amazon DSP product overview confirms that frequency is available as a campaign-level measurement metric and control.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers driving shoppers toward an Amazon Standard Identification Number (ASIN) detail page or a Store, over-frequency on a small retargeting audience is the most common form of wasted spend. A user who viewed the ASIN three times without purchasing is unlikely to convert on impression twenty. You may want to set an order-level cap and pair it with a recency exclusion on users who have already viewed the product page a defined number of times.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers driving off-Amazon traffic, the Amazon Ad Tag pixel enables retargeting of site visitors. Without an order-level cap, a small site-visitor audience can receive impressions from every line item in the order simultaneously, and frequency climbs faster than the audience size would suggest. You may want to set an order-level cap and monitor unique reach relative to total impressions in DSP reporting.

learn_more.source_title: Amazon DSP: Advertise with a demand-side platform

investigate.intro: Open the order in the Amazon DSP console and confirm whether a frequency cap is set at the order level. Then check whether line-item-level caps would allow aggregate exposure to exceed a reasonable ceiling.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Navigate to the order you are auditing and open its settings.
Locate the frequency cap section at the order level. Note whether a cap is present and what the time window and impression limit are.
Open each active line item under the order and note whether it carries its own frequency cap.

If no order-level cap exists, add the per-line-item caps together to estimate the maximum impressions a single user could receive from this order in one week. Note that number alongside the number of active line items.

amazon_dsp_reporting.label: Amazon DSP reporting
steps: Pull a reach-and-frequency report for the order over a recent 7-day window.
Look at the distribution of impressions per unique user. If a small segment of users accounts for a large share of total impressions, over-frequency is occurring.
Note the average frequency and the 90th-percentile frequency for the order period.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether an order-level frequency cap is set. An order with a cap that limits impressions per user per week passes. An order with no order-level cap, regardless of line-item caps, is a fail. The practitioner notes the number of active line items and the sum of their individual caps to quantify the potential over-exposure.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the order-level frequency cap status and the sum of line-item caps above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Amazon DSP order for {{site_name}} carries no frequency cap at the order level. Each line item has its own cap, but with multiple line items running against overlapping audiences, a single user can receive impressions from every line item up to each one's individual ceiling. The aggregate exposure per user is higher than any single cap implies, and the excess impressions drive up costs without a proportional increase in reach or conversions. {{voice_recommend}} setting an order-level frequency cap that reflects the total impressions the campaign should deliver per user per week, and treating the line-item caps as secondary controls for specific formats or placements.

audit_checks.title: Recent converters and disqualified audiences are not suppressed from active line items
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Serving ads to users who already converted wastes impressions that could reach new prospects. For endemic advertisers, continuing to retarget a user who purchased an Amazon Standard Identification Number (ASIN) in the last thirty days produces near-zero incremental return while consuming retargeting budget. For non-endemic advertisers, geography and intent mismatches carry the same cost.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP allows advertisers to attach audience exclusions to orders and line items. A suppression list is an audience segment defined so that matching users are excluded from receiving ads. The most useful suppression lists target users who have already taken the desired action, users who fall outside the campaign's geographic footprint, or users whose behavioral signals disqualify them from the offer.

Suppression lists work at two levels. An exclusion at the order level prevents all line items from reaching the excluded audience. An exclusion at the line-item level applies only to that line item, leaving other line items free to reach the same users. For recent-converter suppression, the order level is the appropriate place to attach the exclusion so no line item wastes impressions on the converted segment.

For endemic advertisers, Amazon DSP surfaces purchase-signal audiences that can be inverted into suppression segments: users who purchased the ASIN in a recent window are known to Amazon and can be excluded directly. For non-endemic advertisers, the Amazon Ad Tag pixel builds a converted-visitor audience that serves the same purpose.

A few signs point to missing suppression:
  • No audience exclusions are attached to the order or to any line item
  • Retargeting line items are running but no post-purchase or post-conversion exclusion exists
  • Geographic targeting at the line-item level does not match the campaign's intended footprint, and no geo exclusion is in place to catch the gap
You may want to review the order-level exclusions first, then the line-item exclusions, and confirm that a converted-user audience is attached at the order level for any retargeting-oriented campaign.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, Amazon DSP can target and exclude audiences based on purchase behavior drawn from Amazon's first-party shopping data. You may want to confirm that users who purchased the advertised ASIN in the last 30 days are excluded at the order level, not just at one line item. A 'new-to-brand' conversion goal in particular depends on excluding existing customers, so the suppression list needs to match the attribution window the campaign uses.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers, the Amazon Ad Tag pixel creates a site-visitor audience that can be segmented by page visited or action taken. You may want to confirm that users who completed the goal action, such as a form submission or phone call, are excluded from the retargeting line items through an audience derived from the conversion event rather than the general site-visitor pool.

learn_more.source_title: Amazon DSP: Advertise with a demand-side platform
A complete guide to Amazon Marketing Cloud

investigate.intro: Review the order's audience exclusions and confirm that a suppression audience exists for recent converters. Check whether the exclusion is applied at the order level or only at individual line items.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the order settings and navigate to the audience targeting section.
Look for exclusion audiences attached at the order level. Note whether any segment covers recent purchasers, recent converters, or disqualified geographies.
Open each active retargeting line item and check its audience exclusions separately. Note whether any exclusions here are missing from the order level.
If no exclusion audiences exist anywhere on the order or its line items, note the absence and the type of campaign (retargeting or prospecting) for the evaluation.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: If the advertiser has access to Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC), run an overlap query between the retargeting audience and a recent-converter segment.
Note the percentage of retargeting impressions being served to users who already converted in the attribution window. A high overlap confirms that suppression is missing or incomplete.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a suppression audience for recent converters is attached at the order level. An order with a post-conversion exclusion applied at the order level passes. An order with no exclusions, or with exclusions only at individual line items that leave other line items unsuppressed, is a fail recorded with the campaign type and the missing exclusion type.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the order-level and line-item exclusion audiences above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP order is running retargeting line items without a suppression audience for recent converters at the order level. Users who already completed the conversion goal remain eligible to receive ads from every line item in the order, which directs retargeting budget toward an audience that has already acted rather than toward new prospects. {{voice_recommend}} attaching a post-conversion exclusion audience at the order level so that all line items inherit the suppression, and reviewing whether any geographic or intent-based exclusions are also missing.

audit_checks.title: Contradictory or overly narrow targeting layers prevent the line item from spending
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A line item with contradictory targeting filters, or with so many stacked audience, geography, and placement restrictions that the eligible inventory is near zero, delivers few or no impressions. The budget sits unused and the campaign's goal goes unmet. The impact depends on how much of the order's budget is allocated to the affected line item.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP line items combine multiple targeting dimensions: audience segments, geographic targets, supply type (e.g., streaming TV (STV), online video, display), device type, and deal or placement restrictions. When these layers are applied with 'AND' logic, each addition narrows the eligible pool further. A line item that targets a specific Amazon audience segment, restricts to a single metro area, limits to STV supply only, and also includes a private marketplace (PMP) deal list may find that no available inventory satisfies all conditions simultaneously.

The result is a line item that is active in the console but delivers near-zero impressions. Budget allocated to it goes unspent while other line items in the order may be over-serving to compensate.

Amazon DSP does not always surface a warning when a line item's targeting combination is too restrictive to deliver, so the signal is in the delivery data rather than a console alert. A few patterns to look for:
  • A line item shows a very low impression count relative to its budget and bid, despite being active for several days
  • Spend is concentrated on a small number of line items while others in the same order show near-zero delivery
  • The line item combines an audience exclusion with the same audience used as an inclusion, which zeroes out the eligible pool entirely
You may want to review each low-delivery line item's targeting combination one layer at a time to identify which restriction is eliminating the available inventory.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, a line item targeting an Amazon audience for a specific product category while also restricting to a private deal for premium placements may find that the deal does not carry the audience data needed to match. You may want to confirm that the deal inventory overlaps with the audience segment before combining them as hard requirements.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers, STV-only line items that also carry narrow geographic targets and a small first-party pixel audience can produce a targeting combination that is theoretically valid but practically undeliverable at the bid the line item sets. You may want to widen one layer, such as expanding the geo or adding open-auction supply alongside the deal list, and observe whether delivery recovers.

learn_more.source_title: Amazon DSP: Advertise with a demand-side platform

investigate.intro: Identify line items with low or zero delivery relative to their budget and bid, then review their targeting combinations to find the layer or layers eliminating available inventory.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the order's line-item view and sort by impressions delivered over the last 7 days.
Identify any line items that are active and have budget remaining but show impressions significantly below what the budget and bid would imply at normal delivery rates.
Open each low-delivery line item and review its targeting settings layer by layer: audience inclusions, audience exclusions, geography, supply type, device type, and deal lists.
Check whether any inclusion audience and exclusion audience overlap, which would zero the eligible pool. Note each targeting dimension that could be narrowing delivery.

amazon_dsp_reporting.label: Amazon DSP reporting
steps: Pull an impressions report broken down by line item for the order.
Compare impressions delivered to budget consumed per line item. A line item spending near zero despite available budget is a candidate for the targeting-contradiction check.
If available, review the supply type and audience breakdowns to confirm whether any single layer accounts for the delivery gap.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per line item, whether delivery is running at a reasonable rate relative to budget and bid. A line item delivering at a pace consistent with its budget and bid passes. A line item that has been active for 7 or more days, has budget remaining, and has delivered fewer than 10% of the impressions its budget would imply at floor CPM is a fail recorded with the targeting combination identified as the likely constraint.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the delivery rate and targeting combination for each low-delivery line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more line items in the {{site_name}} Amazon DSP order are active but delivering near-zero impressions relative to their allocated budget. The targeting combinations on these line items stack audience, geographic, supply-type, or deal restrictions in a way that leaves little or no eligible inventory at the bid and budget set. Budget assigned to these line items is not reaching users. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the targeting layers on each underdelivering line item, loosening the most restrictive dimension first, and confirming that no audience inclusion and exclusion pair overlaps to zero out the eligible pool.
